lessons.title: Automation Fundamentals: Triggers, Actions, and the If-Then-Else Stack
lessons.title_es: Fundamentos de Automatización: Disparadores, Acciones y la Pila Si-Entonces-Sino
lessons.description: Build your first production automation using the trigger-action-condition model and avoid the five logic errors that break workflows in production.
lessons.description_es: Construye tu primera automatización de producción usando el modelo disparador-acción-condición y evita los cinco errores de lógica que rompen los flujos de trabajo.
lessons.content:
The Trigger-Action-Condition Model

Every automation — from a simple email notification to a multi-step agent workflow — is built from three primitives: Triggers (what starts the workflow), Conditions (if-then-else logic that routes the flow), and Actions (what the workflow does). Get these three concepts solid before touching any automation platform.

Triggers are events: a form is submitted, a contact's lead score crosses a threshold, a payment fails, a Slack message contains a keyword, a calendar event starts. Every workflow starts with exactly one trigger. If you think you need two triggers, you need two workflows.

Conditions are binary tests applied to data: contact.industry == 'legal', deal.value > 5000, email.opened == false. Conditions create branches. Use them to route different contact segments to different paths instead of building separate workflows for each case.

Actions are the outputs: send email, create CRM record, add to sequence, post Slack message, call API endpoint, update field value, wait N days, branch on condition result.

The 5 Logic Errors That Break Production Workflows

  • Error 1 — No loop guard: A workflow that creates a record also triggers on record creation. Result: infinite loop. Always add a condition that exits if a flag field is already set.
  • Error 2 — Missing null checks: Your action reads contact.company_name but 30% of contacts have no company. Result: workflow fails silently on those contacts. Add an exists() check before reading optional fields.
  • Error 3 — Race conditions: Two workflows fire simultaneously on the same contact and both try to update the same field. Result: unpredictable field values. Serialize with a wait step or use field-level locking.
  • Error 4 — No error path: API call fails, workflow halts. Result: contact is stuck in limbo, no one knows. Always add a failure branch that alerts a human via Slack or email.
  • Error 5 — Untested edge cases: You test with your own email address (which is perfect data). Real contacts have missing fields, unusual characters in names, and non-standard phone formats. Test with intentionally dirty data before going live.

Building Your First Workflow: Lead Handoff

Trigger: Form submitted (demo request). Conditions: if deal.value > 5000 → route to senior rep; else → route to junior rep. Actions: create deal in CRM at Qualified stage, assign to correct rep, send internal Slack notification with contact summary, enroll contact in 5-touch nurture sequence, set follow-up task for rep due in 24 hours. This single workflow replaces 20 minutes of manual work per lead.

lessons.title: AI Agents vs. Workflows: When to Use Which
lessons.title_es: Agentes de IA vs. Flujos de Trabajo: Cuándo Usar Cada Uno
lessons.description: Understand the architectural difference between deterministic workflows and AI agents, and design the right tool for each use case.
lessons.description_es: Comprende la diferencia arquitectónica entre flujos de trabajo deterministas y agentes de IA, y diseña la herramienta correcta para cada caso de uso.
lessons.content:
Deterministic vs. Probabilistic Automation

The most important architectural decision in automation is whether your process needs to be deterministic (same input always produces the same output) or probabilistic (AI judgment is needed because inputs vary unpredictably). Use the wrong model and you either over-engineer a simple process or under-deliver on a complex one.

Use a workflow (deterministic) when:

  • The inputs are structured and predictable (form fields, database values, API responses)
  • The output is a specific action with no ambiguity (send this email, create this record, post this message)
  • You need 100% auditability and reproducibility
  • The process runs more than 100 times per day (LLM costs compound fast)

Use an AI agent (probabilistic) when:

  • The input is unstructured text (email body, call transcript, support ticket free text)
  • The task requires judgment, classification, or summarization
  • Different inputs should produce meaningfully different outputs (personalized email drafts, custom proposals)
  • The action space is dynamic — the agent needs to decide which tool to call based on context

The Agent Architecture Stack

A production AI agent has four layers:

  • Perception layer: What data does the agent read? CRM records, emails, website events, API responses. Define the exact data the agent receives — garbage in, garbage out.
  • Reasoning layer: The LLM prompt that defines the agent's role, rules, and decision framework. This is your most important engineering investment. Spend 80% of your time here.
  • Tool layer: What can the agent do? Send email, query CRM, create task, post to Slack, call external API. Every tool is a function with defined inputs and outputs. The agent calls tools by name.
  • Memory layer: What context does the agent retain across sessions? Short-term (within one conversation), long-term (stored in a database and retrieved by search), and episodic (specific past interactions). Most production agents need all three.

Hybrid Pattern: Workflow + Agent

The most effective production systems combine both: a deterministic workflow handles routing and structured actions; an AI agent handles the tasks that require natural language understanding. Example: workflow triggers when a new support ticket arrives → routes to correct team (deterministic, based on category tag) → AI agent reads the ticket, searches the knowledge base, and drafts a reply → human reviews and sends. The workflow handles the plumbing; the agent handles the thinking.

lessons.title: Prompt Engineering for Production: System Prompts, Few-Shot Examples, and Output Schemas
lessons.title_es: Ingeniería de Prompts para Producción: Prompts de Sistema, Ejemplos y Esquemas de Salida
lessons.description: Write system prompts that produce consistent, parseable outputs every time — the foundation of any reliable AI automation.
lessons.description_es: Escribe prompts de sistema que produzcan resultados consistentes y parseables siempre — la base de cualquier automatización de IA confiable.
lessons.content:
Why Most AI Automations Break in Production

AI automations fail in production for one reason: the prompt is not specific enough to produce consistent, parseable output across all edge cases. Your automation works in testing (with clean, typical data) and breaks when a contact writes their message in Spanish, omits their company name, or includes a quoted email thread in their support ticket.

The fix is a production prompt architecture with four mandatory sections: Role, Rules, Output Schema, and Examples.

The Four-Section System Prompt

Section 1 — Role: One sentence defining who the AI is and its primary goal. Be specific. Not "You are a helpful assistant" — write "You are a CRM data extraction agent. Your only job is to extract structured contact data from unstructured email text and return it as valid JSON."

Section 2 — Rules: A numbered list of hard constraints the AI must follow. These handle edge cases. Example rules for a lead qualification agent:

  • If company name is not mentioned, set company to null. Do not infer or guess.
  • If the email is in Spanish, extract data exactly as written. Do not translate names or company names.
  • If multiple phone numbers are mentioned, use the first one only.
  • If you cannot determine the intent of the email with confidence > 0.8, set intent to "unclear" and do not attempt classification.

Section 3 — Output Schema: Define the exact JSON structure you expect. Include field names, types, and allowed values for enums. The AI should never return free text when JSON is expected. Example:

{"contact_name": "string", "company": "string|null", "intent": "demo_request|pricing_inquiry|support|unclear", "urgency": "high|medium|low", "summary": "string max 100 chars"}

Section 4 — Few-Shot Examples: Provide 2–3 input/output pairs showing the AI exactly what you expect. Include at least one edge case (missing data, ambiguous intent). Few-shot examples are the single highest-leverage improvement you can make to a production prompt — they cut output variance by 60–70%.

Testing Your Prompt

Before deploying any AI prompt to production: run it against 20 real inputs from your data. Measure: JSON parse success rate (target 100%), field accuracy (compare against human-labeled ground truth), and edge case handling (inputs with missing data, unusual formats, non-English text). If parse success rate is below 99%, add output schema enforcement to your API call — use structured outputs / function calling mode in the Claude API.

lessons.title: Building a 24/7 Lead Qualification Agent: End-to-End Implementation
lessons.title_es: Construyendo un Agente de Calificación de Leads 24/7: Implementación de Principio a Fin
lessons.description: Build a complete lead qualification agent that reads form submissions, scores leads, drafts personalized replies, and creates CRM records — without human intervention.
lessons.description_es: Construye un agente completo de calificación de leads que lee envíos de formularios, puntúa leads, redacta respuestas personalizadas y crea registros en el CRM, sin intervención humana.
lessons.content:
System Architecture

This lesson walks through a production lead qualification agent that processes every inbound form submission in under 60 seconds, 24 hours a day. The architecture has five components working in sequence: Trigger → Data Enrichment → AI Qualification → CRM Action → Response.

Step 1 — Trigger: Webhook on Form Submission

Configure your form to POST to a webhook URL when submitted. The payload includes all form fields plus metadata (timestamp, source URL, UTM parameters). Your automation platform receives this webhook and starts the workflow. Store the raw payload immediately — before any processing — so you can replay the event if something fails downstream.

Step 2 — Data Enrichment (30 seconds)

Before the AI sees the lead, enrich it with external data. Call two APIs in parallel:

  • Clearbit/Apollo: Pull company size, industry, revenue, tech stack, LinkedIn URL using the email domain. This turns "John at ACME" into "John Smith, CTO at ACME Corp (Series B SaaS, 150 employees, uses Salesforce + HubSpot)".
  • Email verification: Confirm the email address is valid and deliverable. If invalid, skip the AI step and route to a "bad data" queue for manual review.

Step 3 — AI Qualification

Pass the enriched lead to the AI agent. The agent returns a JSON object with: qualification_score (0–100), intent classification, suggested pipeline stage, recommended first response tone (warm/professional/technical), and a draft reply email personalized to the lead's company and inferred use case.

Qualification score thresholds: 0–30 = nurture sequence only, 31–60 = junior rep assigned, 61–100 = senior rep assigned + immediate Slack alert.

Step 4 — CRM Action

Based on the AI output: create a Contact record, create a Deal at the appropriate pipeline stage, assign to the correct rep (round-robin within tier), set all enrichment fields, add the AI summary as a CRM note, and create a follow-up task due in 4 business hours.

Step 5 — Response

For scores above 31: send the AI-drafted reply from the assigned rep's email address within 5 minutes of form submission. Speed-to-lead is the single highest-impact variable in conversion rates — responding in under 5 minutes increases conversion by 8x vs. responding in 30 minutes (MIT/InsideSales research). Queue the email in the rep's outbox for a 2-minute review window before it sends. If the rep takes no action in 2 minutes, it sends automatically.
